coredata.dc:title: A sustainable synthesis of green carbon quantum dot (CQD) from Catharanthus roseus (white flowering plant) leaves and investigation of its dual fluorescence responsive behavior in multi-ion detection and biological applications
coredata.dc:creator: Arumugham, Thanigaivelan
Alagumuthu, Manikandan
Amimodu, Reshika Gnanamoorthi
Munusamy, Sathishkumar
Iyer, Sathiyanarayanan Kulathu
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we have presented a facile, efficient and green strategy to prepare fluorescence CQD for multi ion sensing and bio imaging application. Hydrothermal carbonization technique was adopted as one-pot synthetic method to prepare CQD, using Catharanthus roseus (white) leaves as carbon source without any passivating and oxidizing agent. The as-synthesized carbon hold significant advantages such as high fluorescence (FL) emission, high aqueous solubility, stability and non-toxicity etc. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) confirmed CQD's surfaces enriched with oxygen and nitrogen composition. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) revealed that average of size of CQD was around 5 nm. The maximum quantum yield of as prepared CQD was 28.2% at excitation wavelength of 330 nm. The influence of different pH affecting on fluorescence behavior of CQD was systematically studied. In multi ions sensing, FL enhancement and quenching was identified as dual FL responsive mechanism in selective and sensitive detection of Al3+ (Linear range (LR) = 0–6 μM and Limit of Detection (LOD) = 0.5 μM) and Fe3+ (Linear range (LR) = 0–6 μM and Limit of Detection (LOD) = 0.3 μM) ions, respectively. Additionally, CQD exhibited its antioxidant activity and its potential to be used in bio imaging against MCF-7 cells. MTT assay showed good biocompatibility results. Finally, the proposed Catharanthus roseus (white) leaves derived CQD may exhibit great promise as sensing probe for cancer diagnosis and treatment and also multi- ions (Al3+ and Fe3+) in field of environmental water analysis.

head.ce:title: A sustainable synthesis of green carbon quantum dot (CQD) from Catharanthus roseus (white flowering plant) leaves and investigation of its dual fluorescence responsive behavior in multi-ion detection and biological applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we have presented a facile, efficient and green strategy to prepare fluorescence CQD for multi ion sensing and bio imaging application. Hydrothermal carbonization technique was adopted as one-pot synthetic method to prepare CQD, using Catharanthus roseus (white) leaves as carbon source without any passivating and oxidizing agent. The as-synthesized carbon hold significant advantages such as high fluorescence (FL) emission, high aqueous solubility, stability and non-toxicity etc. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) confirmed CQD's surfaces enriched with oxygen and nitrogen composition. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) revealed that average of size of CQD was around 5 nm. The maximum quantum yield of as prepared CQD was 28.2% at excitation wavelength of 330 nm. The influence of different pH affecting on fluorescence behavior of CQD was systematically studied. In multi ions sensing, FL enhancement and quenching was identified as dual FL responsive mechanism in selective and sensitive detection of Al3+ (Linear range (LR) = 0–6 μM and Limit of Detection (LOD) = 0.5 μM) and Fe3+ (Linear range (LR) = 0–6 μM and Limit of Detection (LOD) = 0.3 μM) ions, respectively. Additionally, CQD exhibited its antioxidant activity and its potential to be used in bio imaging against MCF-7 cells. MTT assay showed good biocompatibility results. Finally, the proposed Catharanthus roseus (white) leaves derived CQD may exhibit great promise as sensing probe for cancer diagnosis and treatment and also multi- ions (Al3+ and Fe3+) in field of environmental water analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, Fluorescent sensor materials based on aggregation-induced emission (AIE) is leading edge of the rapidly developing field and have opened a new era of analytical techniques [1–4], when compared to some traditional analytical techniques such as atomic absorption spectrophotometer and inductively coupled plasma spectrometry [5]. The current researches in the field of aggregation-induced emission molecules enables researchers to develop various types of sensing materials such as fluorescence (FL) organic material [6–9], oligomeric FL materials [10], polymeric FL material [11,12], metal organic frame work (MOF) based FL material [13], supramolecular frame work (MOF) based FL material [14] and carbon quantum dots (CQD) [15] for various advance numerous fields, including toxic heavy metal sensing: for both cations and [16–19] and anions [20], neurotransmitter field [21], drug delivery [22] and bio imaging applications [23,24]. Apart from carbon quantum dots (CQD), most of the organic sensing moieties could be prepared through non-sustainable route, which needs more tedious fabrication steps such as azide-alkyne click reaction [25], thiol-yne click reaction [26], thiol-ene click reaction [27], cationic ring opening polymerization [28], polymer functionalization [29–31], photo-initiated RAFT [32] and polycoupling reactions [33]. Moreover, most of the fluorescent organic materials are hydrophobic in nature, exhibit weak interaction between them and that causes instability in complex physiological environments [29]. Therefore, it is highly essential to explore the most stable AIE based hydrophilic fluorescent materials [34].

Carbon quantum dots (CQD) is one of the emerging nanomaterial in the carbonaceous nano structures family. Due to their unique chemical and physical properties such as excellent fluorescence, high aqueous solubility biocompatibility and nontoxicity, carbon quantum dots (CQD) have broad spectrum of applications which includes chemo sensor [15], biosensor [35], cell imaging [36], drug delivery [37], solar cells [38], and catalysis [39]. Several synthetic techniques of CQD have been reported such as pyrolysis [40], hydrothermal carbonization method [41], microwave irradiation [42], laser ablation [43], arc discharge [44], electrochemical exfoliation [45], plasma treatment [46] and acidic oxidation [47]. Among them, hydrothermal carbonization method is considered as a green synthetic approach because of its feasible process, use of aqueous medium and preparation of CQD with better quantum yield.

A large number of recent literatures reveled that serious efforts are being made to fabricate the simple and sensitive CQD fluorescent probe metal ion sensing and other various applications. For example, Sachdev et.al 2015 reported a facile one-step hydrothermal treatment of coriander leaves for preparing carbon dots (CDs) for sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ ions [48]. The research work of Shangguan et.al 2016 reported that using citric acid and basic fuchsin as reaction source, the carbon dots with dual emissions at 475 and 545 nm were synthesized through a one-pot hydrothermal procedure and employed as a new promising candidate for intracellular pH sensing [49]. Dong et.al 2012 designed for Cu2+ ion detection based on the quenched fluorescence (FL) signal of branched poly(ethylenimine) (BPEI)-functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [50].Travlou et.al 2018 prepared S- and N-doped carbon quantum dots using a simple hydrothermal treatment of S- or N-containing organic compounds/polymers and proved its capability to act as bactericides against Gram-positive (B. subtilis) and Gram-negative (E. coli) bacterial strains [51]. These research work convey that the effect of synthesis route, functional group of precursor, functionalization and doping of hetero atom influencing strongly the quantum yield and chemical functionality of the synthesized carbon dots. Nature always receives more attention from material scientists because of its limitless source for developing novel materials with less environmental impact. In order to develop biomass derived CQD, various bio-waste/plant materials including orange juice [52], gelatin [53], coffee grounds [36], bamboo leaves [54], sweet potato [55], watermelon peel [56], banana plant [57], capsicum [58] and milk [59] were used as renewable natural precursors to synthesize the carbon dot material. In view of these excellent FL and sensing properties, biomass derived CQD, has been receiving tremendous attention among researchers as an alternative material for semiconductor quantum dots and other fluorescent organic moieties.

Catharanthus roseus, it is also well known as Madagascar, C. roseus is a species of flowering plant belonging to the family Apocynaceae. The native of C. roseus is Indian Ocean island of Madagascar and now they are widely cultivated in China, Indonesia, Australia, North and South America. The plant leaves contains monomeric indole alkaloids: vindoline and catharanthine as most abundant constituents, which possess remarkable medicinal values such as antibacterial, antifungal, antibiotic, antioxidant, wound healing and antiviral activities. Moreover, the extraction of plant leaves is a rich source of pharmaceutically important terpenoid indole alkaloids and it is currently used in semisynthesis of two anticancer drugs (vinorelbine and vinflunine) in pharmaceutical industries [60,61]. Therefore, it is highly desirable to prepare the efficient AIE based CQDs from Catharanthus roseus (white flowering plant) leaves without aid of strong acid solvent, surface passivation reagent and complex post-treatment process.

In the present research contribution, we have mainly focused on eco-friendly and green synthetic approach to prepare carbon quantum dot (CQD) without any passivation agent by facile hydrothermal method as one pot strategy using Catharanthus roseus leaves as carbon source. The chemical functional group, size and photophysical properties of CQD were characterized by FTIR,

HR-TEM, UV and FL. Importantly, our results revealed that Catharanthus roseus based CQD have high quantum yield, photostability, biocompatibility and excellent radical scavenging activity (RSA). Moreover, the highly water soluble CQD demonstrated its dual responsive sensing behavior against Al3+ and Fe3+ ions. The final obtained fluorescent sensing probe, Catharanthus roseus based CQD has potential to be used in environmental pollution abatement applications.

Catharanthus roseus (white flowering plant) was collected from the place of Kangeyanallur village, Vellore district of Tamil Nadu, India. Other chemicals are purchased from Sigma- Aldrich. The 0.01 M of phosphate buffered saline (PBS) at pH 7.4 were used to prepare the different pH (3, 5, 7, 9 and 11) of phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) by adding a concentrated phosphoric acid solution or sodium hydroxide solution. Deionized water was used in synthesis step and all experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon quantum dot from Catharanthus roseus (white flowering plant) leaves
ce:section.ce:para: Hydrothermal carbonization technique was opted to synthesis the fluorescent CQD from the Catharanthus roseus (white flowering plant) leaves as a carbon source (Fig. 1 ). Before performing synthesis, the collected Catharanthus roseus (white flowering plant) leaves was washed thoroughly in deionized water, chopped into small pieces and dried at room temperature. 0.5 g of Catharanthus roseus (white flowering plant) leaves was added into 50 mL of deionized water, transferred into Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave with 100 mL inner volume and heated at constant temperature of 200 °C for 4 h. After that, it was allowed to reach room temperature. The pale yellowish solution was obtained and filtered through 0.45 μm membrane filter to remove the unreacted organic moieties and unwanted large particles. Finally, the purified fluorescent CQD was collected in the clean glass vial and stored at 5 °C for further characterization and experiments.

Various analytical techniques were performed for obtained CQD including Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, shimadzu IR affinity-1 instrument) to prove the functional groups, UV–Visible absorbance spectroscopy (Perkin Elmer) to fix excitation wavelength and 200 KV FEI-Tecnai G2 20 S-TWIN High resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) to examine the size and shape of CQD. The fluorescence emission properties was tested by using a HITACHI 2573–001 fluorescence spectrometer.

To determine the Quantum yield, previously established procedure was followed by comparing their integrated PL intensities. The reference sample “Quinine sulfate” in 0.1 M H2SO4 (quantum yield 54%) was selected as a standard and absorption of both reference and CQD was kept below 0.10 in the 10 mm fluorescence cuvette to minimize re-absorption effects. The following equation was applied to calculate the quantum yield of CQD sample
(1)

where, the code “R” and “CQD” represents the reference and sample, respectively. The parameter “??” refers the quantum yield, I represents the integrated fluoresce emission intensity, “A” refers the absorbance value at exited wavelength and “Ƞ” denotes the refractive index of solvent medium (refractive index η of water = 1.33).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Fe 3+ and Al 3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical procedure, stock solutions of different metal salts were prepared for concentration of 100 μM by using deionized water. The 2 mL of 10-fold diluted CQD was taken in cuvette and measured maximum fluorescence intensity as F0 (Blank). After that, each metal ions with 100 μM was added and incubated for 5 min before recording fluorescence spectrum at excitation wavelength of 330 nm. The recorded fluorescence spectrum was used to identify the highly selective metal ions. Finally, Limit of Detection (LOD) experiment was carried out for three times at lower concentrations (1–6 μM) for highly sensed metal ions (Fe3+ and Al3+) and results of quenching efficiency of CQD with Fe3+ and fluorescence enhancement of CQD with Al3+ are fitted to Stern–Volmer equation, F0/F = Ksv [X] +1 and F/F0 = Ksv [X] +1, respectively. Where, F0 is fluorescence intensity without metal ions, F is fluorescence intensity with metal ions, Ksv slope of the linear fit and [X] is concentration of the metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of antioxidant activity or radical scavenging activity (RSA)
ce:section.ce:para:
2,2-diphenyl- 1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) free radical assay was carried out to determine the antioxidant potential of CQD [62]. Exactly, 100 μL of various concentration of CQD was added into the 2 mL of 100 μM methanolic solution of DPPH, mixed thoroughly to attain uniform concentration. All the samples with appropriate controls were incubated in dark for 30 min and then absorbance was recorded at 517 nm. The below equation was employed to calculate the percentage antioxidant or radical scavenging activity of CQD,
(2)

CQD =

where AD and AS are the absorbance of the DPPH solution without CQD and DPPH solution (sample) with CQD, respectively.

Michigan Cancer Foundation-7 (MCF-7) is the most common breast cancer cell line that promotes most frequent malignancy in females. So, it is essential to explore more on fluorescence cell imaging probe of those cells for cancer diagnosis and treatment. The normal breast epithelial (MCF-10a) and early passage of breast cancer (MCF-7) cells were selected for cytotoxicity experiment and these cells were developed and cultured as previously described [63,64]. For the MTT (3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide) assay, the cells were cultured in 96-well, flat-bottom titer plates at a density of approximately 5000 cells per well and incubated with different dosage of CQD (0.1 to 1 mg/mL) for 12 h at 37 °C in 5% CO2 atmosphere. After completion of incubation, the medium was subsequently removed and the cells were washed with phosphate- buffered solution (PBS). Further, 15 μL of MTT dye (5 mg/mL) in DMEM (Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium) media was added and incubated for 4 h. Finally, the medium was discarded and 150 μL of DMSO was then added to dissolve the purple colour formazan crystals. The absorbance was measured at 570 nm and the cell viability [mean (%) ± SEM, n = 3] was expressed as follows,
(3)
Cell viability
%
=
A sample
A control
× 100

Before performing the in vitro bio imaging studies, the MCF-7 cells were cultured in the growth medium, which was supplemented with 10% of fetal bovine serum and 1% of penicillin–streptomycin mixture with and without 100 or 500 μL of the CQD. Then, the cells were allowed to grow in a CO2 incubator at 37 °C and 5% CO2 for 5 h. After completion of incubation period, phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) was used by two time to wash the cells and subsequently in vitro bio imaging analysis was carried out using confocal fluorescence microscopy (Weswox-FM3000 Imaging System) under ultraviolet excitation.

Catharanthus roseus (white flowering plant) is an excellent natural precursor material for synthesis of carbon quantum dot (CQD) due to the abundant elemental source of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen in leaves. One step hydrothermal synthesis route resulted pale yellowish colour of CQD solution after successful carbonization of Catharanthus roseus (white flowering plant) leaves. The measured pH value of CQD solution was about 6 and it suggesting the presence of –OH & –COOH functional groups on the surfaces of CQD.

The Fig. 2a shows the interpreted peaks of FT-IR spectra as an evidence to confirm the chemical functionality of CQD. The broad peak of around 3334 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching of O-H(as) and the peak at 2918–2853 cm−1 was corresponding to the aliphatic C-H(as) and C-H(s). Moreover, the C=O(s) group was identified from the peaks at 1693 cm−1 and 1365 cm−1. The aromatic C=C(s) bond was confirmed by the peak at 1595 cm−1 and C-N(s) bond exhibits peak at 1442 cm−1. Furthermore, the peaks located at 1245, 1103 and 1020 cm−1 could be assigned to the C-O(s) and C-O(b), respectively. UV–visible absorption spectroscopy was carried out to examine the optical properties of prepared CQD and shown in Fig. 2b The UV − visible spectrum exhibited broad absorption peaks under 270 and 350 nm, which confirms the n–π* and π–π* electronic transitions due to the presence of CO and CC functional group in CQD. A bright greenish blue colour fluorescence was emitted from CQD sample under the illumination of UV light at 365 nm, which could be seen with naked eye.

The size and shape of the CQD was analyzed by using HRTEM (shown in Fig. 3 ). The obtained TEM images showed that the average size of CQD is ~5 nm, which are in spherical shape and well dispersed in solution. We also identified the elemental composition of CQD using Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX). Fig. S1 shows EDX image of CQD. The characteristic EDX peaks of CQD indicating the major exciting elements like Carbon (C, wt. 98.14%), Oxygen (O, wt. 0.73%) and Nitrogen (N, wt. 1.13%) as a support for FT-IR result (Fig. 2a). Thus, these results could be a strong evidence for the presence of chemical functional groups such as acid, keto, hydroxyl and amine on the CQD surfaces.

To explore the fluorescence properties of CQD in detail, excitation wavelength was altered from 270 to 350 nm with interval of 10 nm. The maximum emission (λem) of 405 nm was noticed at excitation wavelength (??ex) of 330 nm (shown in Fig. 4a,b). Further, the quantum yield (QY) of CQD was calculated to be 28.2% by using Quinine sulfate as a reference (??ex = 370 nm, QY = 54%) in aqueous medium at room temperature. Photostability is one of the important optical properties for fluorescent probes [65,66]. Therefore, CQD was examined for its photostability before and after irradiation at 365 nm by using UV lamp for 1 h and obtained results are displayed in Fig. 5 Importantly, no obvious changes in fluorescence intensity was observed for irradiated CQD from non-iradiated CQD [67]. This results further confirm that CQD possesses excellent photostability. The effect of pH was investigated at different pH = 3, 5, 7, 9 and 11 in PBS and obtained results are shown in Fig. S2. Overall FL emission profile shows that the fluorescence emission at??em = 405 nm was high at acidic pH compared to neutral and basic pH. Especially, maximum FL peak was observed at pH = 3 due to the coverage of abundant O–H and COOH functional moieties on the surface of CQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective detection of detection of Fe3+ and Al3+
ce:section.ce:para: Herein, as-synthesized CQD was employed as fluorescence probe to test the response with different metal ions (K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cr3+, Mn2+,Fe2+, Fe3+,Co2+,Ni2+, Cu2+,Ag+,Zn2+,Cd2+ and Hg2+ along with Al3+). In this experiment, 100 μm of each metal ions was added with 2 mL of CQD (10 times diluted sample) solution and fluorescence (FL) spectrum was documented at 405 nm. Besides, the metal ions, Fe3+ and Al3+ shows strong influence on fluorescence intensity of CQD, while other metal ions exhibits negligible effect on fluorescence intensity (Shown in Fig. 6 ). In addition, the interfering tests for CQD were investigated with most common interfering cations (100 μM) such as K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cr3+, Mn2+,Fe2+,Co2+,Ni2+, Cu2+,Ag+,Zn2+,Cd2+ and Hg2+ along with Al3+ or Fe3+ (100 μM) and obtained results were displayed in Fig. 7a and b. The results confirmed that the addition of interfering cations did not cause any significant impact on Al3+ or Fe3+ detection.

To examine the sensitivity of CQD on concentrations of Fe3+ [48], the fluorescence quenching efficiency was explored by using Stern–Volmer, F0/F = 1 + KSV [Q] plot (a strong linear plot, R2 = 0.9969) in the concentration range 1–6 μM (Fig. 11a and b), Where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of CQD without and with of Fe3+ ions, [Q] depicts the concentration of Fe3+ and Ksv = 0.02993 (slope of the linear fit) is a quenching constant. Limit of detection (LOD) of Fe3+ was measured to be 0.3 μM based on the following equation,
(4)
Limit of Dection
LOD
=
3 σ
S
Where σ is the standard deviation of blank signal (n = 3) and m is the slope of the linear fit.

Some of the researches prove the antioxidant potential of hydrothermally synthesized CQD. DPPH (2,2-diphenyl-1-picryl-hydrazyl-hydrate) assay is the most common free radical method to evaluate the radical scavenging or antioxidant activity of CQD. Fig. 12 shows the results of radical scavenging or antioxidant activity of CQD concentration ranging from 50 to 300 μg/mL. Interestingly, it was observed that radical scavenging or antioxidant activity gradually increases with the increase of concentration of CQD. Despite the radical scavenging or antioxidant activity.

of CQD is dosage dependent, it attains the saturation limit after 200 μg/mL and it was estimated 67.3%. Moreover, no obvious changes in antioxidant activity was observed with further addition of CQD. This is because of that the DPPH was transformed into stable DPPH-H complex by accepting a hydrogen radical. It can be noticed by changing colour of the solution from deep purple to light yellow. From the curve, the amount of antioxidant necessary to decrease the concentration of DPPH by 50% is calculated to be 131.9 μg mL−1. The reason for antioxidant properties of Catharanthus roseus derived CQD is understood from the previous research [71]. Environmental stresses such as UV and temperature on Catharanthus roseus leaves may show the remarkable impacts on plant phytochemicals constituents (terpenoid indole alkaloids, catharanthine, vinblastine, vincristine and vindoline). The constituents in damaged plants were found to play a major role in increasing the antioxidant properties of CQD. The antioxidant activity of as prepared CQD were compared with other research works [72–76] in Table S1. In comparison with other research works, the results of antioxidant activity of as prepared CQD are satisfactory by exhibiting the efficient radical scavenging properties.

MTT assay was performed to explore the cytotoxicity effects of CQD and changes in cell viability are displayed in Fig. 13 . First, in the case of MCF-7, it can be noticed that no significant decline in cell viability up to the CQD concentration of 0.5 mg mL−1 with respect to control. However, the increasing CQD concentration to 1 mg mL−1 minimal decreases survival rate of cell, which was around 88% (±2.1). As same MCF-10a, it can be observed that percentage of cell viability was found to be around 82% (±3.5) when increasing dosage of CQD from 0.1 to 1 mg mL−1. Over all, these results clearly demonstrated that prepared CQD shows non-toxicity up to 0.5 mg mL−1 in both MCF-7and MCF- 10a cells. Thus, this study conclude that CQD derived from Catharanthus roseus (white flowering plant) leaves had good biocompatibility behavior against MCF-7 and MCF- 10a cells. In addition, exclusively fluorescence bio-imaging of MCF-7 cells was carried out with CQD and obtained images are displayed in Fig. 14 . No fluorescence emission was observed for cells without CQD. Further, the addition of different concentration of CQD with MCF-7 cells exhibits the difference in the fluorescence image. The 500 μL of the CQD shows bright fluorescence cell images when compared to 100 μL of the CQD due to the high penetration CQD into the MCF-7 cells.

The present research work successfully demonstrated a green and facile approach to synthesize eco-friendly fluorescence probe, CQD via hydrothermal carbonization of Catharanthus roseus (white flowering plant) leaves as natural precursor without any chemical additives and complicated post treatment. As synthesized CQD with size of ~5 nm exhibited the chemical functional groups (acid, keto, hydroxyl and amine groups) present on the CQD surfaces. Particularly, they exhibited maximum quantum yield of 28.2% with good photostability and the interesting pH dependent behavior at the excitation wavelength of 330 nm for CQD. Meanwhile, these CQD successfully employed as a fluorescence probe for sensing the Al3+ and Fe3+ ions through chelation enhanced and chelation quenched fluorescence mechanism, respectively. Moreover, Catharanthus roseus (white flowering plant) leaves derived CQD revealed a favorable antioxidant or radical scavenging activity and low cytotoxicity. Bioimaging studies proved its diffusion ability through the MCF-7 cells without any decay of fluorescence emission. From these attractive results, it can be concluded that Catharanthus roseus (white flowering plant) leaves derived CQD could be a new type fluorescence probe which may provide promising potential in the field of environmental pollution abatement and cancer treatment research.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
